quoteFirst: The full realisation of their predicament came with the dusk. The electric lights were shut off! Jane, crawling into bed tearfully at
quoteLast: , turned the reading light switch over her head, but no flood of rosy radiance poured down on the hand-embroidered pillow with the pink bow.
title: Love Stories
author: Rinehart, Mary Roberts, 1876-1958

quoteLast: a numerous crowd made its appearance coming from the town. Among them walked with a firm step, a large black man, dressed in a long white frock, white pantaloons, and a white cap with a long peak which fell backward on his shoulders.
title: Letters of a Traveller; Or, Notes of Things Seen in Europe and America

quoteFirst: At 9:30 p. m. the train left Cologne for Berlin. Near Berlin the British inspector of passports told me: "I allow you this time to pass this way to Lithuania if you promise me not to return to America the same way." I said: "I will return by way of Libow." At
quoteLast: the train arrived in Berlin and left for Koenigsberg and Eytkunen at 9:30 p. m.
title: Thirty Days in Lithuania in 1919
author: Saurusaitis, Peter

quoteFirst: On the 9th of August, there was much noise and confusion throughout Paris; and it became known that an insurrection was to take place the next morning.  Louis knew that something was dreaded, but he slept as usual.  His servant, Clery, put him to bed at
quoteLast: , while it was still daylight, and then went out, to try what he could learn of the proceedings of the people.  The king and queen supped at nine o'clock. While Madame Campan waited on them at table, a noise was heard outside the door.  Madame Campan went to see what it was.
title: The Peasant and the Prince
author: Martineau, Harriet, 1802-1876; Kronheim, Joseph Martin, 1810-1896 [Illustrator]

quoteFirst: Rhoda found her life for the first week or two sufficiently comfortable. Mr. Ferrand was away, and Isabella, the daughter, was at school from
quoteLast: to four. The rest of the time she either studied or practised on the piano. She was a pretty, amiable girl, but Rhoda thought she seemed very languid and indifferent. Mrs.
title: Rhoda's education
author: Guernsey, Lucy Ellen, 1826-1899

quoteFirst: This I propose to do, with your consent, this evening. It is now twenty-five minutes past eight by the true time, as my watch has it. At
quoteLast: exactly I shall have the honor of bidding you good evening, Miss Cynthia Badlam, whether you give me those papers or not. I shall go to the office of Jacob Penhallow, Esquire, and there make one of two communications to him; to wit, these papers and the facts connected therewith, or another statement, the nature of which you may perhaps conjecture.
title:
The Atlantic Monthly, Volume 20, No. 120, October, 1867
A Magazine of Literature, Science, Art, and Politics
author: Various

quoteFirst: _15th._--The regiment marched from Klip Drift at
quoteLast: Strong force of the enemy located, holding strong positions. The brigade came under shell fire from the left flank, but this fire was silenced, after considerable loss, by the R.H.A.
title: The History of the 2nd Dragoons: "Royal Scots Greys"

quoteFirst: At half-past five in the afternoon of this day we embarked, going toward the north, with a few turns to the northeast, according to the bends of the river. We may have traveled eleven miles when we anchored at
quoteLast: at the point where the land of the Ompines ends, having skirted its shore all day.[37]
title: Colonial Expeditions to the Interior of California Central Valley, 1800-1820
author: Cook, Sherburne Friend, 1896-1974

quoteFirst: Here, then, I threw myself upon the sofa, and, being alone, reflected a few moments on the manner of escaping which had occurred to me. The physician had arrived a little before, at
quoteLast: ; and I had now to accompany him, as usual, from bed to bed, with pen, ink, and paper, to write down his prescriptions for the direction of the old nun, who was to see them administered. What I wrote that evening, I cannot now recollect, as my mind was uncommonly agitated; but my customary way was to note down briefly his orders in this manner:
title:
Awful Disclosures of the Hotel Dieu Nunnery of Montreal
Containing, Also, Many Incidents Never before Published
author: Monk, Maria, 1816-1849

quoteFirst: Emmie kept on going to bed at
quoteLast: , even when she was old enough to have struck for another hour's freedom. But Emmie had not so much to get into her day; in fact, she was constantly going about saying she had nothing to do, and begging her grandmother to find her some way of getting through the hours.
title: The Open Question: A Tale of Two Temperaments
author: Robins, Elizabeth, 1862-1952
